text.content:
Svellheim Downtime Projects

These are narrative downtime activities for your campaign. They are not tied to any system mechanic — the GM decides how long projects take and what resources are needed based on the fiction.

How to Use These Projects

  • When a player wants to pursue a project during downtime, find the relevant entry below.
  • Use the description and prerequisites as narrative guidance — what does the character need to gather, who do they need to talk to?
  • The "Downtime Days" value is a rough guideline. Adjust based on your campaign pacing and the player's approach.
  • Projects can be completed in stages across multiple downtime periods.
  • Consider requiring ability checks (Cunning, Cunning, Smith's Tools, etc.) for key milestones, with DC based on project complexity.

Crafting Projects

Novice

Askmål Hearth-Lamp

The pine forests of the Askeskog do not frighten easily, but they respect a steady light. The firekeeper families of Askmål have long understood that oil runs out and fire is borrowed — so the best lamp is one that asks little and gives much. The hearth-lamp is sealed against wind, its glass ground to a slow amber diffusion, and its wick treated with a resinous compound that draws on the lantern's own warmth to sustain itself. It is not magic, strictly speaking. It is very good craft.

This project produces a sealed glass-and-iron lantern in the Askmål firekeeper tradition, treated internally with a pine-resin sustaining compound. The lantern burns at a steady low flame for up to 12 hours without consuming any oil, fed by the residual heat cycling of the resin compound. After 12 hours, one flask of oil may be added to reset the cycle. The lamp cannot be blown out by natural weather and the amber glass prevents night-vision disruption at range.

Rules Note: Burns for 12 hours without oil. Immune to wind extinguishing. Creatures outside 5 meters do not suffer the blinded condition from looking toward the lamplight. After 12 hours, requires 1 oil flask to recharge.

Dverg Camp-Iron

A proper Dverg has opinions about tools. Strong opinions. The kind of opinions that get recorded in a workbook and referenced in arguments forty years later. The camp-iron is one piece of that argument — borne as proof that a traveler need not carry ten tools when one properly made one will serve. The tongs are the spike, the spike is the knife, the knife folds back into the hook. The fire striker is always the fire striker, because some things should not be clever.

This project produces a compact folding multi-tool forged in the Dverg journeyman tradition: a field knife, a hooked trowel, a driving spike, and an integrated fire-striker. The camp-iron fits in a belt pouch and functions as a proper tool for any of its components' uses. Heroes using this tool gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Crafting tests when improvising repairs, building camp structures, or starting fires in difficult conditions.

Rules Note: Functions as a knife (light weapon, improvised), a trowel, a spike, and a reliable fire-striker. Grants an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Reason tests involving improvised fieldcraft, camp construction, or equipment repair when no other tools are available.

Fjordmål Trade-Rope

The rope-walkers of Fjordmål know that the sea does not forgive sloppy work. Every coil is tarred twice, measured against the walker's own arm, and then whispered over — a short asking in the old tongue, that the rope remember it was once a living thing and should try to keep the bearer living too.

This project produces a coil of 20 meters of Fjordmål trade rope, treated with pine tar and threaded with a single loop of buoyancy-rune cord. The finished rope has markedly greater grip in wet conditions and provides genuine resistance to saltwater rot. A hero bearing this rope gains an second chance (two rolls, take best) on tests to climb, rig, or secure loads in wet or maritime environments. Additionally, if the hero falls into water while the rope is fastened to them, they do not immediately sink — the buoyancy cord keeps their head above water for up to one minute without effort.

Rules Note: Grants an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Might and Agility tests involving climbing, rigging, or rope-securing in wet environments. If attached when falling into water, the bearer floats automatically for 1 minute.

Headsman's Blade

The old executioner's swords were never made to hack — they were made to read. A true headsman studies the condemned before the stroke falls: the set of the shoulders, the tension in the neck, the way weight shifts when hope leaves the body. The best blades remember those lessons. When you strike with a headsman's blade, the second chance (two rolls, take best) finds the seam in your enemy's guard and holds it open for all to see. Those who fight beside you know exactly where to aim. And those who hear the blade's whisper — a low, rasping voice that murmurs "bend the knee" — know that mercy was offered, and refused.

Headsman's Mark

1/turn. When you hit a creature with this weapon, the target is marked for death until the end of the target's next turn. While marked, all attacks against the creature have an second chance (two rolls, take best).

The blade emits a barely audible whisper — a small, cold voice that says "Bend the knee." Only the wielder and the struck creature can hear it.

Rules Note: Second Chance (two rolls, take best) on all attacks against the marked creature. Lasts until the end of the target's next turn. Triggers automatically on hit, 1/turn, no combat action cost. Does not stack with itself.

Tonttu Snare-Set

Tonttu do not fight large things directly if they can arrange for large things to trip, dangle, and embarrass themselves. The snare-art is taught quietly, passed through households under the name "pest-work," and only the very inattentive fail to notice that the same knot that catches a rat would take a man's ankle just as cleanly.

This project produces a set of six snares following traditional Tonttu trap-making methods. The snares are constructed from lightweight cord, bent wood, and a trigger pin of compressed bark — small, low-profile, and very patient. When set by a hero with knowledge of the technique, each snare triggers on a creature of Small size or larger that crosses the trigger-line. A caught creature is immediately restrained until they free themselves. The snares can be reset and reused unless damaged.

Rules Note: A set of 6 Tonttu snares. Any creature of Small size or larger that crosses a deployed snare's trigger must succeed on an Agility test (difficulty set at time of crafting, default Medium) or become Restrained until they free themselves with a Might test.

Master

Beinmark March-Drum

Every Beinmark column moves to the drum. The march-master sets the pace — three beats: push, hold, breathe — and a trained unit can cover twice the ground a rabble manages, arriving in better condition. The drum itself is half the art. The hides must be true, the shell resonant, the tone carrying over wind without going shrill. A bad drum loses the column. A good drum keeps it.

This project produces a full-size march-drum in the Beinmark military tradition, constructed of smoked oak and rune-cured hide stretched over turned wooden hoops. When played by a hero who knows the march-rhythms (learned during the project), the drum's cadence provides a measurable cognitive anchor for overland movement. Units of up to 12 people travelling with the drum active can sustain a forced march pace for an additional hour per extended rest without accumulating fatigue penalties. Heroes within earshot of the drum gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Toughness tests made to resist exhaustion during overland travel.

Rules Note: When beaten during overland travel, up to 12 listeners gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Toughness tests against march exhaustion and may extend forced march by 1 hour/day. The bearer must have both hands to play and cannot use the drum and a weapon simultaneously.

Gråtstrand Salt-Cure Kit

Gråtstrand fishwives know precisely how long any given catch will last. Three days unsalted, ten days half-brined, three months properly packed in a sealed cask with coarse sea salt and a cord of dried kelp. The kit is not magic. It is the result of several hundred years of people learning what happens when you eat badly-preserved fish.

This project produces a full salt-cure kit following the Gråtstrand preservation tradition: a set of 8 wax-sealed ceramic crocks, 2 kilograms of coarse sea salt, a bundle of dried kelp (used to regulate moisture), and a laminated instruction card in both Fjordmål and common tongue. When used properly, the kit can preserve up to 10 full portions of food (meat, fish, or produce) for up to six months without requiring refrigeration or magical intervention. The kit is reusable for multiple cycles as long as salt is replenished.

Rules Note: Preserves 10 food portions for up to 6 months without spoilage. The ceramic crocks are airtight when sealed. Refilling requires 0.5 kg of coarse salt per cycle. The kit itself lasts indefinitely unless the crocks are broken.

Myrmål Reed-Raft

The Myrmål bog-wardens move through their territory without dry land underfoot for days at a time. The raft is not a vessel of pride — it is a tool, made from what the bog provides, bound tight enough to hold what you bring, flat enough to not tip when you are leaning over the second chance (two rolls, take best) to read the bottom. You make it, you use it, and when you are done, you let the bog have it back.

This project produces a flat-bottomed bog-raft following the Myrmål reed-binding tradition. The raft is constructed of thick marsh-reed bundles lashed with tarred cord over a frame of split green-wood, designed specifically for shallow, still, or slow-moving water. The raft can carry up to three adult passengers and their gear, draws only 15 centimeters of water, and can be poled or paddled with improvised equipment. With poles, it moves at a modest but reliable pace through bog terrain that would be impassable on foot.

Rules Note: Carries up to 3 passengers and their gear. Draws 15 cm of water — passable over boggy ground a horse cannot cross. Movement speed: one hex per round when poled (or use Agility test to push harder). Lasts approximately 3 months of regular use before reeds need replacement.

Skjoldfjellsmål Storm-Lantern

The passes above Skjoldfjell have weather that behaves as though it is personally irritated. A torch lasts four seconds. An ordinary lantern is next. Skjoldfjell lamplighters long ago solved this problem through the application of copper, thick glass, a very specific valve arrangement, and a sealed oil reservoir that feeds by gravity and doesn't care what the wind is doing.

This project produces a Skjoldfjellsmål storm-lantern: a copper-body lantern with thick borosilicate-equivalent glass panels, a gravity-fed oil reservoir, and a wind-locking valve system. The lantern's flame cannot be extinguished by any natural wind, including severe mountain gales. It burns standard lamp oil at a normal rate (6 hours per flask). The copper body retains heat, and in cold environments the lantern also serves as a rudimentary hand-warmer. Heroes using the lantern in harsh weather do not have their light source disrupted by environmental conditions.

Rules Note: Cannot be extinguished by wind of any natural strength. Burns oil at normal rate (6 hours/flask). In temperatures below freezing, grants +1 to Toughness tests against cold exposure when held. Immune to rain extinguishing due to sealed valve design.

Veteran (level 9)

Askeskogsmål Root-Shelter

The wardens of the Askeskog do not build their shelters. They grow them — or more precisely, they ask politely and then help the process along with tools and knowledge of the wood's preferences. The root-shelter collapses into a wrapped bundle of living rods no longer than a walking staff. Planted correctly and spoken to properly, it rises in about an hour into something that looks, from inside, astonishingly like shelter.

This project produces a set of 12 living sapling-rods treated with a growth-essence preparation and bound with growth-coded cord. When planted in earth and the activation words spoken (learned during construction), the rods grow over one hour into an interlocked dome shelter roughly 4 meters in diameter, sealed against wind and rain. The interior remains at a consistent temperature several degrees warmer than outside. Sleeping inside the root-shelter counts as a proper haven rest regardless of external conditions — hostile weather, light rain, and ordinary cold do not impose penalties. The shelter can be collapsed and replanted up to three times before the rods exhaust their growth potential.

Rules Note: Assembles in 1 hour when planted in soil. Interior seats 4 comfortably, 6 tightly. Sleeping within the Root-Shelter provides full extended rest benefits irrespective of outdoor conditions (not combat). Collapsible and transportable. 3 deployment uses before the wood dies and must be replaced (requiring a new Crafting project).

Hvitvidde Far-North Sled

Beyond the ice-line, horses die and wheels are useless. The Hvitvidde have been traveling north since before anyone was writing down where they went, and the sled is their answer to a world that would like to kill you for moving. The runners are carved whalebone — not because whalebone sounds impressive, but because at sixty below, iron shatters and whalebone bends. The cords are twisted gut. The harness fittings are copper. Everything wrong with iron is solved before you leave.

This project produces a full Hvitvidde traveling sled with whalebone runners, a copper-fitted harness assembly, and tensioned gut bindings. The sled can carry up to 300 kilograms of cargo or 3 passengers lying flat. Its whalebone runners remain functional in temperatures where iron runners would crack. When harnessed to pack animals (or pulled by heroes), the sled imposes no speed penalty on snow, ice, or compacted tundra terrain, and halves the penalty on other difficult terrain. The gut bindings flex under impact — the sled is extremely difficult to break in normal travel conditions.

Rules Note: Capacity: 300 kg / 3 passengers. Whalebone runners: no speed penalty on snow and ice; half penalty on other difficult terrain. Immune to cold-fracture damage in natural temperatures. If a pull-team of 4+ strong creatures is harnessed, moves at full pull-speed over arctic terrain. Exceptionally difficult to break (Hard difficulty Might test required to damage structurally).

Jötuntunga War-Banner

Among the Jötun, a war-banner is not decoration. It carries a True-Name in its weave — the name of the battle-line it belongs to — and the first time it is raised in a genuine fight, something happens to the people who see it. Something old. The fear does not go away, but it stops mattering quite so much. As though the banner had reminded them that they were already part of something that had won before.

This project produces a towering war-banner constructed on a Jötun loom, threaded with the maker's chosen war-name in the Jötuntunga tradition. The banner stands three meters tall and requires a dedicated bearer. When raised in view of allies before or during combat, it acts as a rallying point. All allies who can see the banner raised gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Fear and Morale tests (Director's discretion on applicability) for the duration of the scene. Additionally, the banner marks its position — enemies attempting to disengage toward it must succeed on a Presence test or be compelled to engage with the bearer instead.

Rules Note: Requires a dedicated bearer (can't fight and carry simultaneously without a penalty). Allies in line of sight gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on fear-type tests. Enemies attempting to disengage away from flag-bearers must pass a Presence test (difficulty Hard) or be drawn back into engagement. A war-banner destroyed in battle reduces morale — Director decides the consequence.

Svellspraak Binding-Codex

In the court tongue of Svellheim, a promise is a document. A document is a promise. Somewhere in that loop of formalism lies the Binding-Codex — a book purpose-built to hold oaths that cannot be quietly forgotten. The paper remembers. When two parties sign together in the codex, the ink dries red, and both signatories feel a faint awareness thereafter if the other begins to act against the agreement. Not control. Awareness. The rest is politics.

This project produces a leather-bound volume of 40 vellum pages treated with an enchanted iron-gall compound, fitted with iron clasps. When two or more parties sign an oath within the codex — in blood or witnessed ink — the oath becomes supernaturally binding in the following way: neither party can violate the oath without becoming immediately aware that they are doing so, and the other signatory receives a faint but clear impression that something is wrong with the agreement. This does not prevent betrayal, but it removes the possibility of unconscious drift from the terms.

Rules Note: Oaths recorded and signed within the Binding-Codex cannot be broken unknowingly. Both parties feel a distinct mental impression when a violation begins. Deliberate betrayal is still possible, but both parties are fully aware of it in the moment. The codex holds up to 20 separate oaths. Oaths cannot be erased — only formally released by all signatories.

Research Projects

Novice

Fjordmål Tidal Tables

The Fjordmål coast is not predictable in the way inland people mean "predictable." It is predictable in the way a large, opinionated person is predictable — if you know their patterns, you can work with them, but you have to know their patterns first. The tidal tables are the result of patience, a good watch, and the willingness to talk to every fisherman you can find and then subtract the exaggerations.

This project involves systematic observation and collation of tidal data for a specific stretch of coastline — interviewing local fishermen, cross-referencing harbor records, spending time at the water's second chance (two rolls, take best) at dawn and dusk, and producing a reference document of predicted tides, current shifts, and safe passage windows. The finished tables cover one defined coastal region for a twelve-month cycle.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero possesses accurate tidal tables for one specific coastal region. Within that region, they gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on navigation tests at sea, automatic awareness of dangerous tidal windows (the Director alerts them to hazardous conditions before they become critical), and second chance (two rolls, take best) on tests to find anchorages, fishing grounds, or hidden inlets. Tables remain accurate for 1 year before conditions drift enough to require updating.

Ormstunga Drake-Signs

Drake-callers of the Ormstunga lineages claim that every species announces itself before it attacks — you just have to know what it is saying. The particular sweep of a tail before a lunge. The way the neck-frill flattens when it is choosing a target versus when it is simply afraid. Spend enough time watching the right creature, alive and not trying to eat you, and you begin to understand something richer than taxonomy.

This project involves intensive study of a specific drake species — its behavior patterns, territorial markings, feeding habits, threat displays, and known weaknesses. The study requires proximity to living specimens, consultation with those who know the species (Ormstunga lineage members, hunters, naturalists), and careful field notes. Upon completion, the hero has detailed working knowledge of this one species that goes well beyond general lore.

Rules Note: On completion, choose one drake species. The hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on all Reason-based Lore tests related to that species. If the hero encounters that species in the field, they automatically recognize threat displays (the Director tells them the creature's current disposition) and are not surprised by the first attack unless other conditions apply. This knowledge can be shared with others through a full day's instruction.

Sommerdalsmål Peace-Customs

The valley settlements of Sommerdal have been resolving disputes between themselves for longer than they've had a common word for "outside." Their peace customs are elaborate, specific, and absolutely sincere — they have been tested, and they have worked, and the settlements know this. Arriving without knowing the forms is not an insult, exactly. But it is a data point they will remember when deciding how much to trust you.

This project involves the systematic study of formal peace customs, treaty-rites, and diplomatic etiquette used among the valley settlements of Sommerdal: the correct order of gift-presentation, which phrases indicate which levels of trust, how to formally request arbitration, when silence communicates respect versus when it communicates contempt, and how to read the table settings at a formal negotiation dinner. The hero must spend time with Sommerdal citizens or a reliable account of their practices.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Presence tests when negotiating, mediating, or making formal requests of Sommerdal settlements and their representatives. They also know when protocol has been violated (Director informs them quietly) and can correct a social error before it escalates. First impressions with Sommerdal figures are improved — treat initial Presence difficulty as one step lower.

Tonttumál Ward-Sign Primer

Tonttu households mark their doors, their lintels, their window frames, and occasionally the underside of furniture with small carved or chalked signs that mean, in aggregate, "you are not welcome here." This assumes you can read them. Most people cannot. The signs are not magic, precisely — they are a language of intention, readable by those who know the grammar, and readable also by things that are more sensitive to intention than humans tend to be.

This project involves learning the basic vocabulary of Tonttu ward-sign language under the guidance of a Tonttu practitioner or from careful study of inscribed examples. The research covers the 24 core signs used in household warding — signs for keeping misfortune out, inviting luck in, marking a threshold as claimed, and warning the small unseen things that live in walls that this house knows their names.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero can read and inscribe all 24 basic Tonttu ward-signs. Inscribed ward-signs (on doorframes, windows, etc.) impose an Intuition test (Medium difficulty) on creatures of supernatural origin attempting to pass through without the resident's permission — on a failure, they are aware they are crossing a claimed threshold and must make a deliberate choice to do so. The signs themselves have no compulsive force, only awareness.

Master

Askeskogsmål Grove-Lore

Every old forest has a history that trees remember differently than people do. The grove-lore of the Askeskog is the art of reading that history — where the old paths ran before the canopy closed, where the spirit-names are still spoken at hollow logs, which clearings were once meeting-places, and which were killing-grounds. The forest does not give this up easily. It requires patience, and a certain willingness to simply be present without agenda for a long time.

This project involves deep study of one specific ancient forest: its navigational paths and landmarks, the resident spirits (their locations, dispositions, and names), historical events that occurred within it, and the ecological patterns that can help a traveler move through without disturbing the wrong things. The study requires significant time within the forest itself, consultation with the grove-wardens, and making peace with some uncertainty that will not be resolved by rational inquiry alone.

Rules Note: On completion, choose one specific forest the hero has studied. Within that forest, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on all tests involving navigation, tracking, hiding, and foraging. The Director provides the heroes with the known names and dispositions of the forest's resident spirits. The hero automatically knows when they are approaching the territory of an entity that would react badly to their presence, with enough time to change approach.

Beinmarksmål Border-Cunning

The Beinmark borders have moved. Several times. Violently. The people who live on the frontier know this — you can tell because they bury things with the food, because the oldest houses have hidden rooms, because there is a third gate on the east wall that no one has used in forty years but no one has blocked up either. Understanding a contested border means understanding everything that caused it to contest.

This project involves compiling the military history of a specific contested border region: which engagements occurred and where, what fortifications exist and why, which families control which passes and by what historical right (or violence), what seasonal factors affect military movement, and which grievances remain unresolved. The research combines archival work with veteran interviews and direct ground observation.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero has detailed tactical knowledge of one contested border region. Within that region, they gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on strategy and planning tests involving troop movement, ambush placement, supply line analysis, or negotiation over territorial claims. The Director provides advance context when an scene has a tactical element the hero's knowledge would address. Additionally, the hero can assess a fortification's construction date and probable vulnerabilities with a Reason test (this succeeds automatically for documented sites).

Dvergsmål Deep-Survey

Dverg do not wander underground. They survey. Every pace is measured, every turning noted, every junction assigned a glyph from the Dverg naming-standard that has been in use for longer than the surface settlements have existed. A good deep-survey is a gift to everyone who comes after — it is the argument that Dverg craftsmanship applies to spaces as well as objects.

This project involves systematic mapping and hazard-assessment of a known underground complex or mine-network: passage widths, ceiling heights, junction landmarks, load-bearing strata, water sources, gas-risk zones, structural failure points, and known inhabitants with territories marked. The work requires access to the target complex, ideally with Dverg architectural records as a starting framework, plus original observation to fill in gaps or update outdated records.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero possesses a detailed survey map of a specific underground complex. Within that complex, they gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on navigation tests, never become disoriented, automatically recognize structurally dangerous areas (Director informs them before an event), and know the locations of all previously documented inhabitants' territories. Sharing the map is easy — making a copy counts as a rest activity.

Gråtstrandsmål Wreck-Records

There is a saying along the Gråtstrand coast: the harbor knows what the sea has eaten. Every harbor-master keeps a ledger, some of it official, most of it oral, none of it complete. The wreck-record project is the art of combining all those incomplete accounts into something useful — cross-referencing the harbor registry, the insurance claims, the stories from the salvagers who came back with less than expected, and the stories from the salvagers who came back saying nothing happened.

This project involves compiling and cross-referencing the wreck records for a specific stretch of coastline: documented locations, estimated cargo status, last known salvage attempts, known hazards at each site (currents, depth, hostile occupants), and the names of the ships and their last registered owners (potentially useful for legal claims). The research mines harbor registries, insurance records, and the memories of fishermen and salvagers who work those waters.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero possesses a wreck index for one specific coastal stretch: locations of known wrecks, estimated remaining cargo value, current hazards at each site, and a directory of unclaimed legal salvage rights. The Director confirms which wrecks can be worked and roughly what they contain. The hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on planning tests for salvage operations in that region and knows which sites are currently worked by other salvagers (potential competition or allies).

Veteran (level 9)

Hvittviddemål Far-North Survey

Past the ice-line, existing maps are at best approximate and at worst optimistic. The Hvitvidde who live there do not use maps in the way southerners mean — they navigate by star-patterns, ice-texture, wind-direction, and routes that exist entirely in trained memory passed generation to generation. Extracting that knowledge into a form a southern hero can carry requires the cooperation of those who hold it, and the humility to accept that some of what you write down will be wrong in ways you cannot know yet.

This project involves compiling a comprehensive survey of routes, landmarks, rest-sites, hazards, and seasonal variations through the deep north beyond the ice-line, drawing on Hvitvidde scout knowledge and prior expedition records. The result is the most complete southern-format map and notes that can be made of this territory without simply living there for a generation.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero possesses detailed navigational records for the far north — marked rest-sites, seasonal hazard windows, route options, and emergency protocols. The difficulty of all Vigilant tests in the surveyed region is reduced by one step. The hero cannot become permanently lost in the surveyed region (they can still be delayed, not lost). The survey also identifies sites of potential interest (ruins, creature territories, resources) that the Director can use as adventure hooks.

Jötuntunga True-Names Registry

A Jötun True-Name is not the name a giant tells you when you ask. It is the name they were called the first time they stood on consecrated ground with a war-oath in their mouth. It is the name that will not burn, cannot be forgotten, and binds every oath they have ever made in the same web. Finding these names requires moving through sources that do not like being moved through — old binding-stones, oath-monuments, the memories of elders who know the names exist but are not certain they should tell you.

This project involves researching and authenticating the True-Names of three specific Jötun war-leaders: their names, the oaths those names bind, the specific conditions under which those oaths apply or can be invoked, and any known weaknesses or vulnerabilities created by those oath-bindings. The research requires approaching Jötun oral tradition from the inside — via an elder, an oath-monument, or a deep-archive text that recorded such things.

Strupemål War-Chant Etymology

The war-chants of Strupemål are the oldest continuous text in use anywhere in the known world that has not been significantly modified. Parts of them predate the settlements that now sing them. Tracing their origins requires not just linguistic knowledge but historical archaeology — mapping how specific phrases evolved, which lines were added after which battles, and what the original words, before erosion changed them, were actually saying. The answer, when you get there, is complicated and unexpectedly interesting.

This project involves full etymological and historical tracing of the Strupemål war-chant tradition — its linguistic roots, evolution over historical periods, the specific events each section commemorates, and the ritual and emotional function of each component. The work requires access to Strupemål bardic masters, old texts where they exist, and a willingness to sit with the chants for long periods simply listening.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero has scholar-level knowledge of the Strupemål war-chant tradition. They gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on any Lore test involving Strupemål history, culture, or language. Additionally, when leading or participating in a war-chant in combat or immediately before it, the hero's presence adds credibility that grants an additional +1 die to the normal chant roll (Director's discretion on applicability). This knowledge can also be used to identify referenced historical events or figures in other cultural texts that contain Strupemål borrowings.

Svellspraak Court-Chronicles

The senior clerks of the Svellspraak court do not keep one set of records. They keep three: the official record, the working record, and the record that exists in their own memory that will never be written down at all. This project is the art of getting access to the second and eventually the third kind — which requires either trust that takes years to build, leverage that takes years to acquire, or the particular combination of patience and directness that makes people decide you already know enough that there is no point in hiding the rest.

This project involves intensive research into the sealed and semi-sealed political records of the Svellspraak court: hidden alliances, undisclosed financial relationships, past treaty violations, family grievances with binding legal implications, and the locations of records that have been "lost" by parties who found them inconvenient. The work is slow, dangerous if discovered, and produces material that is genuinely dangerous to possess.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero possesses a dossier of political intelligence regarding the Svellspraak court — specific secrets, leverage points, undisclosed relationships, and buried records, as determined by the Director. This information can be used in negotiation (second chance (two rolls, take best) on Presence tests with court figures when leverage is applicable), in blackmail (Director determines specific outcomes when deployed carefully), or as evidence in formal proceedings. Possessing this dossier raises the hero's threat level with court intelligence services — they may notice.

Other Projects

Novice

Beinmark Foot-Drill

In the Beinmark tradition, endurance is not a gift — it is a debt you pay in advance. The foot-drill begins at dawn, ends when the drill-master is satisfied (which is usually when you can't feel anything below the knee), and is repeated every day until the specific relationship between your breath, your pace, and your willpower has been negotiated into something sustainable. The payoff comes later, in the places where everyone else is sitting down.

This project involves a sustained training regimen in the Beinmark military march tradition: regulated pace-setting, breath control, proper load distribution, the specific cadence counts that allow a body to sustain movement for hours longer than it believes it can, and the psychological discipline of pushing past the first and second walls of fatigue. The training must be conducted with actual marching — there is no shortcut involving sitting still and thinking very hard about walking.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Toughness tests made to resist exhaustion during overland forced marches. They can sustain a forced march pace for one additional hour per extended rest without accumulating fatigue penalties. The second chance (two rolls, take best) also applies to tests against any ongoing environmental fatigue that functions similarly to a forced march (long swims, heavy labor under time pressure, etc.).

Fjordmål Rope-Work

A Fjordmål sailor who cannot tie will not sail for long. The training begins with the seven essential knots and ends, theoretically, when you can tie any of them in the dark, with one hand, while someone is leaning on your back. The climbing component is separate, but it follows the same principle: you practice until failure requires active effort, not circumstance.

This project is a complete training course in the Fjordmål mariner's rope art: the full vocabulary of load-bearing knots and their appropriate applications, the specific stroke and grip pattern for ascending and descending rope under load, the rigging techniques for attaching loads to uncertain surfaces, and the emergency protocols when a line fails mid-ascent. Training occurs on ships when available; masts, trees, and constructed frames serve when ships are not.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Agility tests involving rope climbing, rigging, load-securing, or knotwork. They automatically succeed on tasks involving standard knot-tying (no test required for simple applications). They can assess a rope's load-bearing capacity and condition with a Reason test (trained eye), and recognize immediately if a rope has been sabotaged or is about to fail. The second chance (two rolls, take best) applies in any environment where ropes or rigging are in use.

Gråtstrand Cold-Swim

Gråtstrand children learn to swim in the sea because the alternative is considerably worse. What they also learn, which outside people often miss, is that cold water is not simply cold water — it is a negotiation. Your body panics first, then bargains, then accepts. The learning is not how to ignore the cold. The learning is how to proceed through the panic to the other side of it with your lungs still working.

This project is a systematic conditioning regimen in the Gråtstrand coastal tradition: repeated cold-water immersion, breath-control exercises specific to shock-response, the swimming stroke adaptations that conserve heat, how to read rip-currents, and the hit die protocols used when coming out of the water before hypothermia can establish itself. The training occurs in actual cold water — there is no dry-land version.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero no longer suffers the Slowed penalty or Toughness-test second chance (two rolls, take worst) from swimming in cold or rough saltwater. They gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on Might tests to swim against strong currents, and they recognize dangerous current patterns automatically when entering ocean water. The conditioning also provides a degree of hypothermia resistance — they can function in cold-water conditions one stage longer than an unconditioned person before temperature begins imposing penalties.

Tonttumál Quiet-Step

Tonttu move quietly because their households move quietly, because the households below are listening, and because the thing on the third step creaks if you stand in the middle and doesn't if you stand at the second chance (two rolls, take best). This is knowledge. Specific, practiced, transferable knowledge. It just takes someone small and patient enough to watch you blunder through the same hallway forty times and then gently explain which forty things you are doing wrong.

This project involves training in the small-folk method of silent movement through inhabited structures: weight management (heel-toe pressure sequencing), breath timing, awareness of floor resonance, the specific way to open a door without producing sound, how to move in the periphery of someone's vision without triggering the reflexive glance, and the rhythm of movement that matches ambient sound well enough to be ignored. The Tonttu call this the "quiet grammar."

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Agility tests to move silently or remain undetected within constructed environments (buildings, ships, caves with flat floors, underground halls). In natural wilderness, the second chance (two rolls, take best) does not apply. The hero automatically knows the quietest path through a known floor plan if they have spent at least a minute studying it. This does not stack with sources of loud equipment unless that equipment is secured against noise.

Master

Dvergsmål Stone-Reading

Dverg who work stone professionally do not think of it as inert. They are not sentimental about this — the stone does not have feelings — but it has structure, and structure has implications, and those implications are readable if you know the grammar. A load-bearing arch will tell you if it is tired. A gas pocket will announce itself three steps before you reach it. Water reads differently in limestone than in granite. The knowledge is entirely practical and saves lives on a regular basis.

This project is a systematic training program in the Dverg art of geological reading: identifying load-bearing versus decorative stonework by visual and percussive analysis, recognizing the gas-seep signatures that precede dangerous underground atmospheres, locating water sources by moisture patterns and mineral staining, distinguishing stable from unstable ceiling rock, and estimating the age and construction quality of underground architecture. The training requires access to actual underground environments across varying geological types.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Reason tests to assess underground hazards: structural saving throws, atmospheric safety, water presence, and construction age/quality. Questions that a non-specialist would require a test for can often be answered by the hero with a brief observation (Director discretion). The hero automatically senses when a ceiling or gallery is at risk of imminent collapse — they feel the minute vibrations and see the dust-tell before anything audible happens.

Myrmål Bog-Scout Fieldcraft

The bog-wardens of Myrmål operate in terrain that most people would describe as "impassable while actively trying to drown you." Their fieldcraft is not romantic — it is the accumulated result of generations of warden-families cataloguing every way the bog kills people and then working out how not to do that. The training covers all of it. By the end, the hero is not comfortable in the bog precisely. But they are no longer afraid of it, which is most of the way there.

This project is an immersive training regimen in the Myrmål bog-warden fieldcraft tradition: identifying safe footing by surface texture and air smell, reading the bubbles and surface movement that indicate unstable ground, the specific movement pattern (weight spread, slow pace, test-before-commit) that allows crossing terrain a heavy boot would sink through, wet-weather fire-starting, and the tracking methods specific to waterlogged terrain where prints fill immediately and you are reading displacement and disturbed vegetation instead.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Vigilant and Cunning tests in boggy, marshy, or wetland terrain. They automatically pass Agility tests to cross soft/unstable ground that would ordinarily require a test (the hero simply moves at half speed instead). They do not get lost in marsh terrain and can read tracks in boggy ground that would be invisible to a non-specialist. These benefits apply in any wetland environment, not only the Myrmål region specifically.

Ormstunga Wyrm-Caller Basics

The Ormstunga lineages claim that every drake has a particular register of sound it listens to — not words, but resonance, the way an instrument responds to the right note struck on another instrument across a room. The wyrm-caller learns to find that register in their own voice, in their posture, in the way they hold still. It does not make drakes friendly. It makes them curious, and curious drake is one that has stopped being categorically hostile, which is a very useful start.

This project is an apprenticeship in the foundational practices of Ormstunga wyrm-calling: the specific vocalization exercises that develop the relevant resonance range, the body-language postures that signal non-threat to drakes of most species, the approach protocol (slow, lateral, eyes-down, no sudden hand movements), and the emergency disengagement signs that have varying utility depending on how hungry the drake is. Training requires proximity to actual, living, non-hostile drakes on multiple occasions.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains ease on their first Presence test when making initial contact with a non-hostile drake of any species. If the test succeeds, the drake treats the hero as a low-threat entity for the remainder of the scene unless circumstances change dramatically. The hero also gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on Intuition tests to read a drake's emotional state before committing to an combat action. These benefits do not apply to drakes that are actively hunting or have already been provoked.

Skjoldfjellsmål Dueling Form

The Skjoldfjellsmål formal duel is a three-act scene: the challenge (verbal, precise, witnessed), the acceptance (formal, binding, timed), and the duel itself — which follows rules so specific that a trained observer can read the whole tactical conversation in the footwork alone. Outsiders who try to engage with the form without knowing it look, to highland eyes, the way someone sounds reading a foreign language phonetically. Competent sound. Wrong meaning entirely.

This project is a study and conditioning program in the Skjoldfjellsmål highland dueling tradition: the full vocabulary of formal stance positions, the transition patterns between them, the specific weight-forward guard used in the highland close style, the rules and etiquette of the challenge-acceptance sequence, and the tactical patience that characterizes the form — which rewards the person who can wait longest for a specific moment rather than the person who hits hardest.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on ability check results when fighting a single opponent in formal or semi-formal single combat conditions (a witnessed duel, a challenge accepted on both sides, or a clearly one-on-one fight where neither side has support). The form's emphasis on patience and precision does not benefit the hero in brawls, group combat, or ambush scenarios. Additionally, the hero is recognized as a trained duelist by the highland clans, which has social implications in Skjoldfjellsmål territory.

Veteran (level 9)

Askeskogsmål Grove-Walking

The grove-wardens of the Askeskogsmål do not navigate by compass. The light tells them direction. The sound of the canopy above indicates wind and weather patterns hours before they arrive. The specific species of lichen on a tree's north face and the angle of its lowest branch tell them soil moisture and slope more precisely than any instrument. The training is not the acquisition of facts — it is the slow development of a different kind of attention, one that requires years to cultivate and does not translate well into instructions that can be written down.

This project is a deep apprenticeship in the Askeskogsmål warden fellowship's art of forest movement: reading canopy light for direction and time, acoustic mapping of terrain through sound-bounce, identifying safe paths through dense undergrowth by observing animal sign, tracking by compressed vegetation and disturbed bark rather than footprints, and the specific quality of awareness — kept wide, not focused — that prevents walking into things that would prefer not to be walked into. The training takes place entirely in forest, over multiple seasons.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero can never become lost in any forested terrain worldwide. They gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on all tracking tests in woodland environments. They automatically detect the presence of large creatures within 30 meters in forest if those creatures are not actively hiding (the Director informs them of presence and general direction). They can read forest terrain well enough to predict ambush positions — when entering a forested area, they identify the best ambush positions with a Reason test (trained eye) before any scene begins.

Jötuntunga Oath-Combat

Among the Jötun, an oath-combat is not merely a fight. It is a legal proceeding. Both parties have named what they are contesting. Witnesses have sworn to report what they see. The outcome is binding in the way that only witnessed decisions can be binding — in the memory of everyone who was present, which is more durable than any document. Training in the form means learning not just the weapon-work, but the vocabulary of formal combat, which has specific words for every stage from challenge to resolution to the declaration of outcome.

This project is a deep apprenticeship in the Jötun tradition of sacred oath-combat: the formal challenge vocabulary, the acceptance protocol, the binding-oath recitation that both parties speak before weapons are raised, the specific restricted techniques (no strikes to the back, no use of ranged weapons, no third-party intervention), and the close-range grapple-and-yield pattern that ends engagements without killing when the outcome is clear. Training requires Jötun instructors and witnessed formal bouts.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero is formally recognized by Jötun culture as eligible to issue or receive an oath-combat challenge — a recognition with significant cultural weight. The hero gains second chance (two rolls, take best) on ability check tests during formal oath-combat engagements (specifically witnessed single-combat under oath). When issuing a formal challenge to a Jötun, the difficulty of the opening Presence test to have the challenge accepted is reduced by one step. Additionally, the hero knows the binding-oath recitation in Jötuntunga and can serve as a legal witness to oath-combats.

Strupemål War-Chant Mastery

The full war-chant tradition of Strupemål is not one chant. It is forty-seven, each with its function: the approach-chant that regulates breath for the final march to contact, the hold-chant used when a line is being pressed and must not give, the grief-chant that begins after the fighting stops and continues until it is finished being true. A master of the tradition is not a singer. They are a living library of controlled collective experience, and in battle, that library matters.

This project requires completing the Strupemål War-Chant Etymology research (or equivalent knowledge of the tradition) before proceeding. It is the practical phase: learning to lead each of the 47 chants with proper breath control, tonal authority, and awareness of how to read the group's state to select the right chant for the moment. The training is part vocal, part martial, and part the kind of quiet sustained attention that becomes second nature only after long practice.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero can lead any Strupemål war-chant. Once per combat scene, the hero may use a movement combat action to begin the appropriate chant — all allies who can hear gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on their next Toughness test. Additionally, the hero can use the approach-chant to grant allies second chance (two rolls, take best) on initiative tests when the group begins a combat with a coordinated advance. The grief-chant, when led after a victory, allows a short 10-minute ceremony that counts as an extended rest for the purpose of recovering one use of a extended rest ability (Director's discretion on applicability). Prerequisite: Strupemål War-Chant Etymology or equivalent.

Svellspraak Court-Tongue

High Svellspraak is not a language in the way common Svellspraak is a language. It is a curated performance of a language — structured, rule-bound, laden with honorific gradations that communicate status, relationship, and political disposition in every word choice. A sentence in high Svellspraak can simultaneously make a statement, imply a question, register a critique, and indicate which family you were educated by, all without the listener being able to prove any of it. Getting that right takes years. Getting it wrong takes no time at all, and is immediately obvious to everyone present.

This project is a total immersion program in the high register of Svellspraak: the complete honorific system, the formal grammar variants that signal court versus administrative versus ecclesiastical context, the vocabulary specific to court proceedings, the written forms used in warrants, contracts, and official correspondence, and the social-register reading that allows a skilled speaker to decode what a court document is actually saying versus what it officially states. The training requires immersion — classroom study is insufficient.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero speaks and reads high-court Svellspraak fluently. They gain second chance (two rolls, take best) on Presence tests in any formal court or noble setting where high register is expected. Words spoken in high Svellspraak carry official weight in formal settings — the hero cannot be mistaken for an uneducated person and is automatically treated as someone deserving a genuine response. The hero can also produce legally valid court documents in proper high-register format (with a Reason test for complex legal formulations). Additionally, they can detect when high-register speech is being used to say something other than what it appears to say — the Director will note when this is occurring.

Armor Imbuing

Novice

Deep-Rune Stitching

Old forge-scripts for binding seams — Dverg masons once used these runic stitches to hold tunnel ceilings together; a smith clever enough to adapt them to plate-rings finds the magic transfers admirably.

Effect: At the start of each scene, you gain full armor 5 until the end of the scene.

Rules: Scene; full armor.

Frost-Quiet Buckles

Travel-clauses from winter road-ledgers — Fjordmål merchants once inscribed silence-promises into their load-fasteners to keep cargo from rattling on ice roads; a hunter repurposed the formula for armor buckles.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on tests made to move quietly in snow, sleet, or heavy rain.

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests.

House-songs of Iron

Hall-carols etched into rivets — an old Svellheim custom, where smiths would sing a house-song as they set each fastening, locking pride and stubbornness into the metal itself.

Effect: Once per extended rest, when you would become prone, you can remain standing instead.

Rules: Prone; Extended Rest.

Saltward Lining

Coastal warding notes from sailmakers — the Gråtstrand coast breeds a peculiar craft tradition: lining armor with salt-pressed seagrass threaded through with brine-proofing scripts, guarding the wearer against corrosion and water.

Effect: You have acid immunity 5. Additionally, you can hold your breath for twice as long as normal.

Rules: full armor.

Master

Amber-Bark Lamellae

Forest-temper instructions on resin-hardening — the wood-crafters of Askeskog developed a technique for laminating bark strips in hardened resin; a clever armorer found the same method seals out poison just as well as moisture.

Effect: You have poison immunity 5, and you gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on saving throws against inhaled hazards.

Rules: full armor; Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Attribute Test.

Bright-Sun Filigree

Sunlit prayer-knotwork copied by shrine-smiths — Ljósamál shrine-smiths work only at midday, etching solar knotwork into armor using focused light rather than fire; the resulting filigree stores warmth that pushes back corruption.

Effect: Once per extended rest, when you would take corruption damage, you gain corruption immunity 5 until the end of the scene and shed faint light for the same duration.

Rules: full armor; Scene; Extended Rest.

Jötun-Pledge Plates

Oath-plates sworn before a longhouse fire — in Jötun tradition, promised armor is forged in ceremony rather than silence; the vow spoken binds the metal against compulsion, and the plates remember.

Effect: Once per scene, when you would be force moved, reduce the forced movement distance by 2 feet (to a minimum of 0).

Rules: Forced Movement; Squares; Scene.

Marsh-Reed Buffer

Wetland craft notes for shock-absorption — Myrmål armor-weavers stuff their padding with treated marsh reeds known to spring back under pressure; the technique adapts naturally to shedding mud from boots and greaves.

Effect: You ignore difficult terrain caused by mud and shallow bogs.

Rules: Difficult Terrain.

Veteran (level 9)

Bone-March Harness

Border-march drill-texts on enduring pain — the Beinmark border troops are legendary for finishing battles they should have lost; this harness carries the cadence of their drill-texts inscribed in iron wire, refusing to let the wearer fall.

Effect: Once per extended rest, when your Toughness would be reduced to 0 or lower, you instead remain at 1 Toughness.

Rules: Toughness; Dying; Extended Rest.

Skyshield Sigils

High-peak wardmarks from stormwatchers — Skjoldfjellsmål stormwatchers live in the high crags where lightning strikes daily; over generations they developed wardmarks that cause bolts and thunderclaps to slide off a warded surface rather than strike it.

Effect: You have lightning immunity 10 and sonic immunity 10. Once per extended rest, when you would be hit by a critical hit, treat it as a normal hit instead.

Rules: full armor; Critical Hit; Extended Rest.

Throat-Oath Gorget

War-chants inscribed as vow-letters — Strupemål war-chanters tattoo vows into their own throats and then transcribe them into armor; the gorget carries the resonance of the chant, pushing back fear the moment it tries to take root.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on saving throws against becoming frightened. Once per scene, when you would become frightened, you can make an immediate attribute test before the condition applies.

Rules: Frightened; Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Attribute Test; Scene.

White-Expanse Mantle

Far-north survival litany stitched into padding — the people of Hvitvidde do not fear the cold; they write litanies into their garments reminding the cold that it does not belong here, and the cold, eventually, agrees.

Effect: You have cold immunity 10, and you ignore mundane environmental cold effects (extreme weather, frozen terrain, ice water, and similar hazards).

Rules: full armor.

Weapon Imbuing

Novice

Dverg Hammer-Truth

Forge-proofs struck into the tang — Dverg forge-masters strike a proof-mark into the tang of every weapon that passes their inspection; the mark is more than a quality stamp — it is a declaration that the weapon is what it claims to be, and nothing can pretend otherwise about the steel.

Effect: This weapon gains the Magic or Psionic keyword (choose when the imbuing is completed). Once per scene, one strike you make with this weapon ignores full armor 5 on the target.

Rules: Magic/Psionic Keyword; full armor; Scene; Strike.

Fjord-Advantage (Second Chance) Balance

Dockmaster's measurements and tide-marks — Fjordmål dockmasters measure the balance of every load against expected wave combat action; they eventually applied the same mathematics to blade-balance, producing weapons that feel steady when the ground beneath them is not.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on strikes made while you are on unstable footing — such as on a boat, ice, rubble, uneven ground, or other precarious surfaces (Director adjudicates).

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Strike.

Marsh-Swallow Temper

Bog-iron notes from wetland smithies — Myrmål bog-iron is smelted differently from mountain iron; it swallows impurities the way a marsh swallows a boot, and when the technique is applied to a weapon's temper, it swallows the resistance of mud and water in the same way.

Effect: You ignore difficult terrain caused by knee-deep water, mud, or bog while wielding this weapon.

Rules: Difficult Terrain.

Tonttu Tripwire Notch

Small-folk trapcraft marks — the Tonttu notch their tools with a finder's mark that makes the tool want to find trouble before trouble finds the holder; on a blade, this becomes an uncanny aptitude for locating pressure-plates and trip-lines.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on tests made to detect or disable mundane traps when you use this weapon as part of your toolkit for the task (Director adjudicates).

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests.

Master

Beinmark Drill-Scar

March-drill cadence cut into the fuller — Beinmark drill-masters notch cadence-marks into training weapons to help soldiers keep rhythm; the marks are never removed when the blade is sharpened for war, and they keep a different rhythm now — the body's own.

Effect: At the start of each scene, you gain temporary Toughness equal to your Might score.

Rules: Temporary Toughness; Scene.

Shieldfell Gale-Runes

Storm-run lines carved by ridge-runners — Skjoldfjellsmål ridge-runners carve directional runes into their equipment to navigate storms; when applied to a weapon, the runes redirect force outward at the moment of impact, throwing the struck target backward.

Effect: Once per turn, when you hit a creature with a strike using this weapon, you can push the target 1 feet (2 m).

Rules: Forced Movement (Push); Squares; Strike.

Sunbright Advantage (Second Chance)

Daylit blessing-lines from open-air shrines — Ljósamál shrine-keepers inscribe blessing-lines into open air at noon, tracing the sun's path with their fingertips; when those lines are etched into a blade, the steel never forgets the warmth and releases it on impact.

Effect: This weapon sheds faint light in a 1-feet (2 m) radius. Once per scene, when you hit a creature with a strike using this weapon, you deal additional fire damage equal to your Reason score.

Rules: Scene; Strike; Damage.

Wyrm-Tongue Challenge

Challenge-phrases copied from wyrm-hunters — Ormstunga wyrm-hunters speak a formal challenge before a kill that compels the creature to fix its attention; the phrase etched into a blade carries the same compulsion, binding a struck enemy's focus to the weapon that struck it.

Effect: Once per scene, when you hit a creature with a strike using this weapon, you can mark it until the end of the scene. Your strikes against a marked creature gain an second chance (two rolls, take best).

Rules: Mark; Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Strike; Scene.

Veteran (level 9)

Ash-Word Sundering

Soot-scribed breaking-words — Askmål fire-readers know words that describe the precise weakness in any structure; inscribed into a blade in soot that never wipes off, the words make the weapon understand what it is breaking against and where to push.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on Might tests made to break mundane objects and barriers with a strike using this weapon. If the object has a breakage threshold, the Director may treat it as one step lower (Director adjudicates).

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests; Strike.

Jötun War-Name

War-names spoken over stone and fire — in Jötuntunga culture, a war-name is given at the moment of greatest crisis and becomes part of the weapon forever; a blade that has been named strikes with certainty the one time that matters most.

Effect: Once per extended rest, your next strike with this weapon automatically scores a critical hit (regardless of the roll result).

Rules: Critical Hit; Strike; Extended Rest.

Summerdell Mercy

Peace-vows taught by valley mediators — Sommerdalsmål valley mediators carry blades that are sworn never to deal the killing blow in anger; the vow is in the hilt-wrap, and the weapon remembers it even in the hands of someone else.

Effect: Once per extended rest, when you reduce a creature to 0 Toughness with a strike from this weapon, you can choose to spare them — they fall unconscious rather than becoming dying or dead. The Director determines the creature's condition and how long unconsciousness lasts.

Rules: Toughness; Dying; Extended Rest.

White-Expanse Severance

Cold-cut geometry from far-north hunters — Hvitvidde hunters must kill cleanly or lose prey in the ice; their blade-geometry is calculated to sever rather than wound, and the cold that is worked into the temper ensures nothing — not magic, not supernatural toughness — can blunt the killing second chance (two rolls, take best).

Effect: Once per scene, one strike you make with this weapon ignores full armor 5 on the target.

Rules: full armor; Strike; Scene.

Implement Imbuing

Novice

Ash-Script Focus

Soot-ink marginalia from hearth-scribes — Askmål hearth-scribes annotate every page of their records in soot-ink that can only be read by firelight; they discovered the ink also reacts to magical and psionic residue, making it invaluable to investigators.

Effect: You gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on tests made to discern magic or psionic influence, to interpret omens, or to recall relevant lore.

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests.

Resonant Claim

Market-oath receipts sealed in wax — Sangøymål market-keepers once pressed their most binding contracts with a waxed seal bearing a single resonant phrase; the implement carries that phrase, letting the wielder claim a second chance at the negotiating table.

Effect: Once per extended rest, reroll a failed test made to negotiate a stated bargain. You must keep the new result.

Rules: Tests; Reroll; Extended Rest.

Summerdell Ink

Soft-vowel glosses from valley storytellers — Sommerdalsmål valley storytellers use an ink pressed from summer flowers that carries the warmth of the growing season in its pigment; a speech made with this implement on hand feels like a day in a meadow, no matter how hostile the crowd.

Effect: Once per extended rest, you can shift the mood of a hostile crowd toward wary neutrality if you have at least a moment to speak. The Director determines how much the crowd's disposition shifts based on your approach and phrasing.

Rules: Director adjudicates; Extended Rest.

Wyrm-Whisper Etching

Scaled phonemes copied from drake-watchers — Ormstunga drake-watchers study the calls of drakes and wyrms for a lifetime; they transcribe the most authoritative phonemes into implements so that others may borrow the weight of that authority.

Effect: Once per scene, you gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on a Presence test made to intimidate, coerce, or bully a creature whose story is tied to wyrms or drakes.

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests; Scene.

Master

Mirror-True Formulae

Counting rhymes used by river-auditors — Fjordmål river-auditors must calculate tolls in darkness, rain, and chaos; they inscribe their counting rhymes into their ledgers and, later, into implements, so the mind stays steady when the eye cannot.

Effect: Once per scene, when a test you make would take a second chance (two rolls, take worst) from poor lighting or distraction, you may ignore that second chance (two rolls, take worst).

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take worst); Tests; Scene.

Shieldfell Countermark

Highland dueling countersigns — Skjoldfjellsmål highland duelists engrave a countersign into their off-hand item at the start of any formal duel, ensuring the item stays in hand no matter what happens; the technique adapts elegantly to implements.

Effect: Once per scene, when an effect would cause you to drop your implement — whether from a forced drop, disarm, damage, or other trigger — you can keep hold of it (Director adjudicates the exact interaction).

Rules: Director adjudicates; Scene.

Tonttu Ward-Knot

Small-folk safety knots and house-signs — the Tonttu tie safety-knots into every object they wish to keep safe; the knots are invisible to those who do not know how to look, and the objects become inexplicably difficult to break, lose, or steal.

Effect: Once per extended rest, you can ward one carried object until the next extended rest. The warded object is unusually resistant to breaking, misplacement, or theft — the Director determines the exact nature of protection, but it should meaningfully complicate attempts to remove it from your possession or destroy it.

Rules: Director adjudicates; Extended Rest.

White-Quiet Invocation

Snowbound prayer-lines memorized by wanderers — Hvitvidde wanderers memorize prayers against cold that quiet the mind as much as the body; when transcribed into an implement, the prayer extends its hush outward, muffling movement briefly.

Effect: Once per scene, you gain cold immunity 5 until the end of the scene. Additionally, you can briefly mute your footsteps until the end of your next turn; during this time your movement makes no sound, regardless of surface (Director adjudicates).

Rules: full armor; Scene.

Veteran (level 9)

Beinmark Oath-Suture

Border-healer sutures and stubborn vows — Beinmark border-healers stitch wounds and vows in the same breath; their suturing thread carries the intent that the patient will not give up, and the implement carries that same refusal forward into magic.

Effect: Once per scene, when you spend a Hit Die, you also gain temporary Toughness equal to your Might score.

Rules: recovery test; Temporary Toughness; Scene.

Gråtstrand Salt-Circle

Coastwise threshold-wards in brine-script — the Gråtstrand tradition of salt-circle warding began as protection against high tides; it became something more when warding-scribes discovered the same patterns kept out other uninvited things.

Effect: Once per extended rest, you can ward a doorway, gate, or threshold until the end of the next scene. Any creature that crosses the warded threshold takes a second chance (two rolls, take worst) on their next ability roll. The Director determines how thresholds are defined in ambiguous situations.

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take worst); Ability Roll; Scene; Extended Rest.

Light-Tongue Benediction

Goldleaf benedictions from open-sky chapels — Ljósamál open-sky chapels are built without roofs deliberately, letting language and light mingle freely; their benedictions, pressed into goldleaf and bound to an implement, pass that openness on through touch.

Effect: Once per extended rest, a creature you touch can either stand up if prone, or end one ongoing effect that would normally end with a attribute test or at the end of their turn (your choice of which).

Rules: Prone; Attribute Test; Extended Rest.

Svellspraak True-Name Margin

Old court annotations kept under seal — the oldest Svellspraak court records contain marginal annotations in a cipher that names every signatory's true circumstances; an implement carrying these annotations can extend that knowing outward, letting the wielder find what wishes to remain hidden.

Effect: Once per extended rest, name a creature you can see. Until your next extended rest, you gain an second chance (two rolls, take best) on all tests made to track, locate, or find information about that creature.

Rules: Second Chance (two rolls, take best); Tests; Extended Rest.
